steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that New York society prefers familiarity over novelty. The correct quotation must show the society actively choosing something familiar when a newer alternative is available — a direct contrast between old and new, with the old winning.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘in metropolises it was “not the thing” to arrive early at the opera’: Describes a social convention about arrival timing. No contrast between old and new, no preference illustrated. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘an exceptionally brilliant audience had gathered to hear her’: Emphasizes the prestige of the audience and the novelty of the performer’s first appearance. This highlights novelty, not a preference for familiarity. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘To come to the Opera in a carriage for hire was almost as honourable’: Discusses how people arrived, not which venue they chose or a preference between old and new. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘though there was already talk… of a new Opera House… the world of fashion was still content to reassemble every winter in the shabby red and gold boxes of… the old Academy’: Directly contrasts a new, grander option with the old venue — and shows society choosing the familiar old Academy anyway. Perfectly illustrates preferring familiarity over novelty. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
